segments.s194-g41: به + noun marks direction/target ('to' or 'at') — here introducing where the blow lands. برگستوان is the target (shoulder) and is the object of the action (lemma list did not supply a matching sense, so it is treated as an attested body-part noun). بر زد is a two-part verbal expression: بر (literally 'upon') combines with زد (past tense 'struck') to mean 'struck upon/at'. کرد چاک is a light verb construction: کرد (past 'made/did') takes the following noun چاک ('a gash') as its object, so 'made a gash.'
segments.s194-g42: گرانمایه (adjective) precedes the noun اسپ ('steed') in the usual adjective–noun order. اندر آمد بخاک is a small clause: اندر (archaic/prepositional 'into/in') with آمد (past 'came') and the directional/completive بخاک ('to the ground') yields 'fell/came to the ground.' The sequence preserves poetic word order (adjective + noun, then verb phrase).
segments.s194-g43: به indicates direction ('to/at') and introduces دربند حصن ('the gate/entrance of the fortress'); دربند here functions as the entrance/gate (not the sense 'captive' or 'pass' in the lemma list) and حصن is 'fortress.' اندر آمد فرود is an inverted verbal phrase: آمد (past 'came') plus فرود (noun/gerund 'descent' used in the compound فرود آمد = 'alighted, dismounted'), so 'alighted at the fortress gate.'
segments.s194-g44: دلیران is the plural nominative 'the brave men.' در here is the noun 'door/gate' (sense 1 in the provided list) and دژ 'citadel/fortress' completes the phrase 'the gate of the citadel.' ببستند is past, 3rd-person plural 'they closed/shut,' and زود follows adverbially to indicate rapidity ('quickly/at once'). The clause thus means 'the brave men quickly shut the gate of the citadel.'
segments.s194-g45: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing the cause/setting. باره here means 'shower/burst' (a rain-like fall), and فراوان qualifies it ('abundant/heavy'). ببارید is the past of باریدن 'to fall (as rain)' so with سنگ (stone) the clause means 'a heavy shower of stones fell' (literally 'from a very abundant shower, stones fell').
segments.s194-g46: بدانست is past tense 'he knew/realized.' کان here functions as a classical connective/particle roughly 'that' (used to introduce the content of the cognition) and نیست is the negative copula 'is not.' جای ('place') + درنگ ('delay; tarry') form the noun phrase 'place/time for delay' — thus بدانست کان نیست جای درنگ = 'he realized that there was no place/time to delay' (i.e. 'no time to hesitate').
segments.s194-g47: خروشید is the past tense (3rd-person singular) of the verb 'to cry out/roar' — here 'Bijan cried/shouted.' The following clause که‌ای نامدار is a vocative: که‌ای (classical/exclamatory 'که ای' ≈ 'O!') addresses someone, and نامدار is the epithet 'famed/renowned.' So the whole segment is 'Bijan shouted: "O famed one!"'.
segments.s194-g48: ز here is the classical prepositional/partitive marker 'of/from' used to introduce attributes or origin (a typical poetic usage). مردی is 'man' (or 'manliness'), and the sequence پیاده دلیر و سوار lists attributes: پیاده 'on foot', دلیر 'brave' (adjective modifying the types), and سوار 'mounted/horseman.' Together the phrase is an appositive/attribute to the addressee: 'of (a) man — brave both on foot and mounted.'
segments.s194-g17: This clause says someone (تو) is not equal to another in fighting. با + او marks the comparison/partner (“with him”), بسنده is an adjectival/adverbial predicate meaning “sufficient / a match,” and نباشی is the 2nd-person present-subjunctive/negative form of بودن used here to express inability or “not being enough.” بجنگ is an infinitival/verb-noun (“in fighting” / “to fight”) that completes the comparative idea: “you are not sufficient with him in fighting.” Word order is emphatic/classical Persian: subject (تو) + comparison phrase (با او) + predicate (بسنده نباشی) + verbal complement (بجنگ).
segments.s194-g18: نگه کن is a two-word imperative: نگه (root noun/verb ‘watch/keep’) + کن (imperative auxiliary ‘do’), meaning “keep watch.” که introduces the following explanatory clause (“for/because/that”), الماس is the direct object “diamond,” دارد is the present-tense verb “has/possesses,” and بچنگ is an adverbial/locative phrase “in (his) grasp.” Note the possessive pronoun is omitted (poetic), so ‘has the diamond in (his) grasp’ is understood.
segments.s194-g19: This short clause places the verb بزد (he struck/shot) first, followed by its object تیر (arrow). بر اسپ is a prepositional phrase ‘at/on the steed’ (بر = at/against + اسپ = horse). The final word فرود here belongs to the next nominal phrase and reads as a verbal/participial idea ‘fell / alighted’: together the clause means ‘he shot an arrow at the horse; Bijan fell.’
segments.s194-g20: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past simple ‘you said.’ باسپ is written as one unit but functions here as با + اسپ (‘with the steed’) — a prepositional phrase; اندرون = ‘inside/within,’ جان = ‘life/soul,’ and نبود is the past negative of بودن, ‘there was not.’ Put together it reports what ‘you said’: ‘you said (that) with the steed there was no life inside.’ Note classical/Persian poetic orthography often compresses prepositional phrases (با + اسپ → باسپ).
segments.s194-g21: بیفتاد is a past-tense verb ‘he fell.’ و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ بیژن is the name ‘Bijan.’ جدا گشت is a light-verb construction meaning ‘became separated / broke away’ (جدا = apart + گشت = turned/became), and ازوی = از + وی ‘from him’ marks the source. Grammatically this is a change-of-state construction: ‘he fell, and Bijan became separated from him.’
segments.s194-g22: سوی is a directional preposition ‘toward,’ followed by تیغ ‘sword.’ با تیغ marks instrumentality ‘with a sword’ (i.e., holding a sword), بنهاد is the past of نهادن ‘placed/set’ (here poetic for ‘turned/laid’), and روی (‘face’) is the object — Persian often omits the possessive pronoun, so روی stands for ‘his face.’ The clause literally means ‘he set his face toward the sword / turned his face to the blade,’ with the instrumental phrase ‘with a sword.’
segments.s194-g23: یکی here is an indefinite ‘one / someone / a certain person.’ نعره زد is a light-verb collocation ‘uttered a cry’ (نعره = shout + زد = he produced/sounded). کای is an archaic vocative particle ‘O,’ addressing the addressee, and سوار دلیر is a noun phrase ‘brave rider.’ Word order is standard poetic Persian: indefinite subject + verb + vocative + epithet.
segments.s194-g24: بمان is an imperative ‘stay/remain.’ تا here introduces a purpose/temporal clause and means ‘until / so that’; it governs the subjunctive-present ببینی ‘that you may see.’ کنون is an adverb ‘now,’ and رزم شیر is a terse possessive phrase ‘the lion’s battle’ (poetic omission of the ezāfe is common). Together: ‘Stay until you see now the lion’s combat.’
segments.s194-g25: ندانی is a 2nd‑person negative/question form meaning “do you not know”; که introduces the subordinate clause. بی‌اسپ is an adjective meaning “without a horse / unhorsed” placed before the noun (classical word order). مردان جنگ is a noun phrase “men/warriors of war” (مردان = men/warriors, جنگ qualifies them).
segments.s194-g26: بیایند is the verb of the subordinate clause (3rd‑pl. present/optative) meaning “they come” or “they may come”; با تیغ هندی is a prepositional phrase “with an Indian sword”; بچنگ here functions as a result/complement meaning roughly “into the (hand‑)grasp / to grips” and links with بیایند to yield “they come to grips (with)”.
segments.s194-g27: ببینی is a 2nd‑person present/future form “you will see.” مرا‌گر is an archaic fused conditional meaning roughly “if (in) my stead” (مرا + گر = ‘if for me/in my place’). بمانی is the 2nd‑person present subjunctive “you remain,” and بجای is the adverbial phrase “in place of / in my stead.” Together they form a conditional: “you will see, if you remain in my place.”
segments.s194-g28: به پیکار is a locative/directional phrase “to/at the battle.” ازین پس is a temporal phrase “from now on / henceforth” (از + این + پس). نیایدت is the negative present/future impersonal/predicative form with the enclitic ت marking the person affected: “will not come to you / will not reach you.” رای is “counsel/plan” here, so the clause means “henceforth your counsel will not come (to the battle).”
segments.s194-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” بیژن is the proper name Bijan. همی is the classical progressive/continuous particle (here marking the action as ongoing). برنگشت is intended as a past action “did not turn back / did not withdraw,” but the provided lemma senses do not include this verbal meaning (so the appropriate sense is not listed). از + فرود here indicates relation ‘from/with Farud’ (but فرود in this passage is the proper name, not the noun “descent,” so the provided noun senses do not match).
segments.s194-g30: فرود here is the proper name (the actor), followed by اندر آن کار “in that affair/matter” (اندر = in/within, آن = that, کار = deed/affair). تندی نمود is a light‑verbal phrase where تندی = haste/fierceness and نمود is the past‑form verb “showed/did” (i.e. “made/showed haste”).
segments.s194-g31: یکی تیر دیگر is an idiom meaning “one (more) arrow / another arrow” (یکی = one/a certain, تیر = arrow, دیگر = another). بیانداخت is a classical past tense verb “threw/loosed” (transitive, taking تیر as object). شیر immediately following is a noun “lion” — in epic poetry a title or epithet — appearing after the verb (poetic inversion) as the actor/epithet.
segments.s194-g32: سپر بر سر آورد is a compact past clause: سپر (shield) is the object, بر سر (on the head) is the adverbial phrase, and آورد is the past verb “brought/put.” Note the poetic inversion placing the subject after the verb: مرد دلیر (the brave/valiant man) is the agent who performed the action.
segments.s194-g1: چنین گفت is a fixed narration formula meaning "thus said" with the verb گفت placed immediately after چنین; شاه جوان is the subject ("the young king") following the verb, showing flexible Old/Poetic Persian word order. با + تخوار marks the addressee: با here functions like "to/with" addressing the lowly person (تخوار).
segments.s194-g2: که introduces the content of what was said (a subordinate/complement clause). آمد is a relative clause head "who has come" (here used as 'who came'). بنوی is an imperative/command "write!" addressing the speaker to record something; یکی نامدار ("a certain renowned one") is the direct object/phrase of that command, with یکی meaning "a certain" and نامدار "renowned." Note the order: imperative verb (بنوی) is followed by the nominal object.
segments.s194-g3: نگه کن ببین stacks two imperatives: نگه کن (literally "hold/look/keep watch") and ببین ("see"). تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause here meaning "so that/in order that." ورا is an old unstressed object/indirect-object pronoun meaning "to him/for him." نام چیست is the question-like predication "what is the name": چی (what) + ست (is) form the copula construction "what is."
segments.s194-g4: بدین is the fused/prepositional phrase (به + این) meaning "to/for this." مرد جنگی is a compound noun phrase "this warlike man/warrior." که introduces a relative clause modifying that man. خواهد + گریست is a periphrastic future: خواهد (auxiliary 'will') + past-stem گریست ('weep') = "will weep/will lament."
segments.s194-g5: بخسرو is a prepositional phrase meaning "to the king" (به خسرو contracted). تخوار سراینده is an appositive noun phrase "the wretched singer/poet." گفت is the reporting verb "said" introducing the following subordinate clause.
segments.s194-g6: که introduces the reported content. این را is the topicalized direct object "this" (را marks definiteness/emphasis). ز ایران means "from/among Iran" (i.e., 'in Iran' or 'of the Iranians'). کسی نیست is the existential negation "there is no one." جفت here is predicative meaning "match/equal (to it)" — i.e., "no one matches this."
segments.s194-g7: که again introduces a relative/explanatory clause. فرزند + [گیو + ست] reads as "is the son of Giv": فرزند (descendant/son) followed by the name گیو with the copula -ست (= است) attached, so the construction means "is Giv's son." مردی دلیر is a predicate nominal "a brave man" describing him.
segments.s194-g8: بهر رزم is a prepositional phrase meaning "in/for battle" (بهر = for/toward, رزم = battle). پیروز باشد uses the verb باشد (classical subjunctive/optative or present copula here) with پیروز as predicate "be victorious" — together giving "will/shall be victorious" or "is victorious" in this poetic register. چو شیر is a simile "like a lion," placed at the end as customary in Persian simile constructions.
segments.s194-g49: 'چنین بازگشتی' is a nominal phrase: 'چنین' ('such/thus') modifies 'بازگشتی' (a poetic/nominalized form of 'بازگشت' = 'retreat/return'). 'و شرمت نبود' follows with the past negative copula 'نبود' ('there was not') stating 'and there was no shame' — an emphatic, archaic way to say he did not turn back in shame.
segments.s194-g50: This clause begins with the interjectional noun 'دریغ' ('pity/alas') and then lists the objects 'آن دل و نام جنگی' ('that heart and the warlike/name of a fighter'). The final word 'فرود' is poetic/elliptical (literally 'descent'/'fall') used as a verbal idea 'was brought low / fell' — classical Persian often omits auxiliary verbs, so 'فرود' supplies the sense 'fell/was humbled'.
segments.s194-g51: 'بیامد' is the simple past 'he came'. It is followed by two prepositional/locative phrases: 'بر طوس' ('to/onto Tūs') and the ablative 'زان رزمگاه' ('from that battlefield'), where 'زان' is an elevated/poetic form of 'از آن' ('from that').
segments.s194-g52: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (classical word order: adverb + verb). What follows is a vocative: 'کای پهلوان سپاه' = 'O champion of the army/host' — 'کای' is an archaic/vocative particle (not the dynastic 'Kay' here), 'پهلوان' is the addressed noun, and 'سپاه' specifies which champion (of the army). The verb 'گفت' is used as simple past 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense).
segments.s194-g53: سزدگر is an impersonal/optative particle meaning 'if it befits' or 'if proper'; برزم is an archaic locative phrase 'in the battle/field' (classical word order puts the locative before the rest); چنین modifies the following noun phrase ('thus'/'such'), and یک دلیر is an indefinite nominal phrase 'one brave (man)'. Together they form the protasis: 'If it befits the battlefield that such a brave one...'.
segments.s194-g54: شود is a subjunctive/optative of شدن 'become' (here 'may become' / 'will become'); نامبردار functions as a predicate adjective 'renowned, named'; یک دشت شیر is a nominal apposition 'a lion of the plain' (یک = indefinite article-like). The clause as a whole states the consequent: '...becomes renowned as a lion of the plain.'
segments.s194-g55: اگر is the conditional 'if'; کوه خارا is a noun phrase 'the hard/flinty mountain' (خارا here descriptive); ز marks source/agent 'from/because of'; پیکان اوی 'his arrows' — اوی is the archaic possessive 'his/her'. The phrase sets the hypothetical: 'If the hard mountain from his arrows...'.
segments.s194-g56: شود is the subjunctive/optative of 'become' ('were to become'); آب و دریا is a parallel noun pair 'water and sea'; بود here functions as an auxiliary/finite copula in the conditional clause ('would be'); کان اوی is a demonstrative/possessive cluster roughly 'that would be his' (کان a classical demonstrative, اوی 'his'). Overall: '...were to become water and sea, that would be his.'
segments.s194-g9: ندارد is third-person singular present of داشتن meaning 'does not have/has not'; جز او = 'except him' (جز is a preposition/particle meaning 'except'). گیو فرزند is an appositive noun phrase 'Giv's son' and نیز ('also') attaches to it to add emphasis or inclusion; the clause is effectively 'he has nothing except him, Giv's son.' The verb governs the following noun phrase which is in apposition to او.
segments.s194-g10: گرامیترستش is a contracted poetic form of گرامی‌تر است + ـش (ـش = 'to him'), i.e. 'is dearer to him.' ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/than' used in comparisons; گنج و ز چیز = 'than treasure and than things' (i.e. than any possessions). The whole segment expresses a comparative: 'he is dearer to him than treasure and things.'
segments.s194-g11: تو اکنون are fronted subject and time adverb ('you now'). سوی is the preposition 'toward'; بارگی دار is a poetic compound ('baragi-dâr') meaning a leafy abode or bower (بارگی 'leafage' modifying دار 'abode'); دست 'hand' is the noun that would be the object of an implied verb. In epic Persian verbs are sometimes omitted for forceful imperatives, so the clause reads as an elliptical command 'you now, (go) toward the leafy bower and (take) the hand.'
segments.s194-g12: دل شاه ایران is a genitive phrase 'the heart of the Shah of Iran' (دلِ شاهِ ایران). نشاید is an impersonal/defective present meaning 'it is not fit' or 'must not'; شکست here is a verbal noun or infinitive-like form meaning 'to be broken' or 'breaking.' Together: 'It is not fitting that the heart of the Shah of Iran be broken' (i.e. 'the Shah's heart must not be broken').
segments.s194-g13: و دیگر 'and moreover/besides' begins the clause; که is the relative/complimentary particle 'that/which'; دارد is present 'he has/possesses'; همی is a poetic adverb (here 'still' or 'indeed'); آن زره = 'that armor.' The clause literally: 'and besides, that armor which he still has.'
segments.s194-g14: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; گیو is the subject; زد is the simple past of زدن 'he struck' (3rd sg. past). بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; میان 'middle' marks the location; برگره (a concrete locative noun) 'on the knot' specifies the precise spot. The clause asks 'Where did Giv strike (it) — on the middle, on the knot?'
segments.s194-g15: برو is an imperative 'go.' تیر و ژوپین lists the items 'arrows and jupin (a padded jacket/armour)'. نیابد is an archaic/poetic present meaning 'does not find' or 'will not find' (negative of یابیدن); گذار here is a noun 'passage' or 'way through.' Put together the phrase says 'arrows and the jupin will not find a passage' (i.e. they will not get through).
segments.s194-g16: سزد‌گر is a conditional/optative particle meaning roughly 'if it is fitting' or 'should it be deemed proper'; پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot' or 'afoot'; کند is the present/subjunctive of کردن 'he should do/make'; کارزار is the object 'battle.' The segment means 'if it be fitting that he make battle on foot' (i.e. 'if it is proper for him to fight on foot').
segments.s194-g61: This segment is a nominal phrase giving the object of the following verbs: a personal name followed by an epithet and the head noun. 'بکین' here functions as an archaic personal name (attached to 'زرسپ' to form the full name), 'زرسپ' is the name proper, 'گرامی' is an epithet meaning 'honored/esteemed', and 'سپاه' is the head noun 'army/host'. In epic Persian names and titles the order and lack of an ezâfe are common (name + epithet + noun) rather than modern spoken Persian's noun + ezâfe + modifier pattern.
segments.s194-g62: 'برآرم' and 'بسازم' are both 1st‑person singular present‑subjunctive forms with the prefixed ب- used in epic Persian to express intention/future ('I will raise' / 'I will make'). The two verbs form a compound predication: the speaker will first 'raise/muster' (برآرم) the army named in the previous segment and then 'make/build' (بسازم) — here 'بسازم' takes 'یکی رزمگاه' as its complement. 'یکی' functions adverbially/quantitatively as 'one' or 'a single', modifying 'رزمگاه' ('battlefield'), so the sense is 'make into a single battlefield.'
segments.s194-g63: This clause is an independent action clause: 'تن ترک' is a juxtaposed noun phrase meaning 'the Turk's body' (epic Persian often omits the ezâfe), 'بدخواه' is an adjective/noun modifier 'the ill‑wishing / hostile one' modifying 'ترک', and 'بیجان کنم' is a causative/resultative construction meaning 'I will make (it) lifeless' — 'کنم' is 1st‑person present‑subjunctive used prospectively ('I will cause/make'). Word order puts the patient ('تن ترک') before the result phrase 'بیجان کنم.'
segments.s194-g64: This clause begins with the preposition 'ز' ('from' / 'by means of'), 'خونش' = 'his blood' (possessive clitic -ش on 'خون'), then the direct object 'دل' ('heart'). The sequence 'سنگ مرجان' is a poetic binomial meaning 'stone and coral' (i.e., a hard, implacable heart); together with 'کنم' (1st‑person subjunctive 'I will make') it yields 'from his blood I will make (my) heart stone and coral.' Note the omitted possessor on 'دل' (understood from context as the speaker's heart) and the use of parallel nouns as a compound epithet.
segments.s194-g33: سپر بر درید is a compact past action: سپر (shield) appears before the verb in classical/poetic order and بر here functions as a local/intensive particle ('against/over' or 'asunder') so together درید (past of دریدن) means 'rent/tore the shield.' The clause و زره را نیافت uses و 'and', then زره را with را as the definite/object marker, and نیافت is ن + یافت (past negated) meaning 'did not find' or 'could not reach/strike the armor.'
segments.s194-g34: ازو 'from him/it' marks the source/target; روی here is 'face' and بیژن is the possessor ('Bijan's face'). The adverbial phrase بپستی ('in a low/abased manner') modifies نتافت, where نتافت is the negative past (ن + تافت) of تافتن 'to turn/turn away' — so the whole segment means 'Bijan did not (even) turn/avert his face low (i.e. he did not shrink back or lower his face before him).' Note the inverted/poetic order (source first) and the negative past construction.
segments.s194-g35: ازان 'from that' or 'thereupon' introduces the circumstance; تند بالا is a compound descriptive phrase ('swift/upward') describing the motion. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', بر سر is a prepositional phrase ('toward the head') and کشید is the past tense verb 'drew/raised' (classical word order places descriptive phrases and the prepositional complement before the verb). The verb کشید here is the past action 'pulled/raised/drew' (note: the supplied lemma senses do not list this meaning).
segments.s194-g36: بزد is the simple past 'he struck/hit'; دست is the direct object 'hand' ('he struck the hand'). The conjunction و links the next action: تیغ از میان برکشید — تیغ 'sword' is the object, از میان 'from between' indicates source, and برکشید is a past transitive verb 'drew up/drew out' (here 'drew his sword out'). Word order places the object phrases before the verb, as common in classical Persian.
segments.s194-g37: فرود here functions as the subject (a proper name or noun), modified by the epithet گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious' placed after the noun (postpositive adjective). ز (poetic from) marks 'from him/it', and بازگشت is the past verbal noun/verb 'returned/turned back' (past action). The clause follows classical prose order with modifiers preceding the verb.
segments.s194-g38: همه باره is an adverbial phrase here meaning 'all at once' or 'at that moment'; دژ 'fortress' is the subject, پرآواز ('full of voice' or 'loud-voiced') is a descriptive adjective placed before the verb, and گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became' (indicating a change of state). The verb at the end marks the result: 'the fortress became loudly vocal/clamorous.'
segments.s194-g39: دوان is a participial/adverbial form 'running/rushed' placed before the subject بیژن (Bijan) for vividness; آمد is the past 'came.' پس پشت اوی is a classical locative phrase 'to/at his rear' (پس = 'after/behind', پشت = 'back, rear') and اوی is the archaic/poetic possessive pronoun 'his,' so the whole means 'Bijan ran and came up behind him.'
segments.s194-g40: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'; تیغ 'sword' is the noun; بد here functions as an intensifying modifier ('very' in context) but that usage is idiomatic/poetic (the supplied dictionary senses do not include this intensifier meaning, so no direct sense matches). تیز is 'sharp', در مشت 'in (his) fist' is a locative phrase, and اوی is the possessive 'his.' The clause describes that he held a certain keen sword in his fist.
segments.s194-g57: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed'; نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not/should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; دارد شگفت literally 'has wonder' — an idiom meaning 'is amazed' or 'feels astonishment'. So: 'The spahbed should not be amazed.'
segments.s194-g58: ازین = از + این 'from/than this' (ازین used as 'from this'); برتر اندازه 'a measure/degree higher/superior' (برتر modifies اندازه); نتوان گرفت is a negative potential construction 'cannot take/seize' — idiomatically 'no further measure can be taken.' The clause means 'no greater measure can be set beyond this.'
segments.s194-g59: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed'; بدارنده is a participle 'holding/possessing' and here modifies سپهبد ('the oath-holding spahbed'); سوگند خورد is the fixed idiom 'took an oath / swore' (خورد in this set phrase means 'took' — an archaic usage).
segments.s194-g60: کزین = ک + ازین 'from this' (killer demonstrative + 'this'); دژ 'fortress' is the object; برآرم is 1st-person singular subjunctive/future of برآوردن 'to lift up / raise' ('I/he will raise'); بخورشید is a classical adverbial phrase 'toward the sun / to the sun' (ب- + خورشید); گرد here is a classical locative/circumstantial 'about/around' or forms part of the idiom indicating motion toward the sun's circle. Together: 'that he would raise this fortress and lift it up to the sun' (poetic hyperbole).

sentences.s194-s21: He struck at the shoulder and made a gash; the noble steed fell to the ground.
sentences.s194-s22: He alighted at the fortress gate; the brave men quickly shut the gate of the citadel.
sentences.s194-s23: A heavy shower of stones fell; he realized there was no time to delay.
sentences.s194-s24: Bijan cried aloud, 'O famed one — brave both on foot and mounted!'
sentences.s194-s9: You are no match for him in combat; keep watch, for he has the diamond in his grasp.
sentences.s194-s10: He shot an arrow at the horse; Bijan fell. You said, ‘with the steed there was no life inside,’
sentences.s194-s11: He fell, and Bijan was separated from him; he turned toward the sword, taking up the blade and set his face to it.
sentences.s194-s12: A man cried out, “O brave rider, stay, that you may now see the lion’s combat.”
sentences.s194-s13: Do you not know that unhorsed warriors in battle come to grips with Indian swords?
sentences.s194-s14: You will see — if you remain in my stead — that from now on your counsel will not come to (the) battle.
sentences.s194-s15: When Bijan did not withdraw from Farud, Farud, in that affair, showed haste.
sentences.s194-s16: One (someone) loosed another arrow; the brave man lifted a shield onto his head.
sentences.s194-s1: Thus the young king said to the wretch: "Write down the name of a certain renowned one who has come."
sentences.s194-s2: Look and see, so that you know what his name is — this warlike man who will weep.
sentences.s194-s3: The wretched singer (poet) said to the king that there is no one in Iran equal to this.
sentences.s194-s4: That he is the son of Giv, a brave man, who will be victorious in battle like a lion.
sentences.s194-s25: There was no such retreat nor any shame; pity for that heart and for the war‑fame that fell.
sentences.s194-s26: He came to Tūs from that battlefield and thus spoke: 'O champion of the host,'
sentences.s194-s27: If it befits the battlefield that one brave man be thus, he will become renowned as a lion of the plain.
sentences.s194-s28: If the hard rock of a mountain, from his arrows, were to turn into water and sea, that would be his.
sentences.s194-s5: He has no one except him; Giv's son is also dearer to him than treasure and other things.
sentences.s194-s6: You now, go toward the leafy bower and take his hand; the heart of the Shah of Iran must not be broken.
sentences.s194-s7: And besides, he still has that armor; where did Giv strike it, at the middle of the knot?
sentences.s194-s8: Go; arrows and the jupin will not find a passage; if it is fitting that he fight on foot.
sentences.s194-s31: I will muster the honored army of Bakin Zarasp and make one battlefield.
sentences.s194-s32: I will make the Turk's body lifeless; from his blood I will make my heart like stone and coral.
sentences.s194-s17: He rent the shield, and did not find the armor; Bijan did not lower his face before him.
sentences.s194-s18: When that swift one reared up, he struck at the hand and drew his sword from between (them).
sentences.s194-s19: The noble Farud turned back from him; at once the whole fortress became loud with sound.
sentences.s194-s20: Running, Bijan came up behind him; in his fist he held a certain, very-sharp sword.
sentences.s194-s29: A spahbed should not be amazed; one cannot take a measure beyond this higher degree.
sentences.s194-s30: The spahbed, holder of an oath, swore that he would raise this fortress and lift it up to the sun.
